body: Why this is safe section in https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Control.TaskSeq/issues/294 has some related verbiage

Please add a test that shows the use of `TaskBuilderBaseBuilder`.

Forgot about this discussion point. These should be `invalidArg`. We throw `ArgumentOutOfRange` if we find that, while iterating, we end up past the last index, for instance. Here, it is clearly an argument error, as there's never an index that's negative.

(this is my interpretation from MS's logic in the same/similar cases for Array/Seq/List)

I'll double-check, but the errors raised should be the same as for similar functions in `Seq.xxx`. I vaguely remember being surprised about this previously. It may be because the argument check is immediate, and the range check happens while iterating the sequence (which, in turn, may have a dynamic length).
argumentoufofrange? I guess you're refactoring, which answers that...

Excellent, thanks!

I'm not immune to your points, but I prefer orthogonality where possible. If there's a `Async.ofTask`, then there's an `Async.toTask` as well. Besides, anybody understanding tasks knows they're hot-started (just like `let! x = async { ... }` starts the async if used inside a task). It's also similar to seq in that `Seq.toArray` consumes the seq, just like `Async.toTask` "consumes" the async.

> in 90% of cases it kicks off work and is not just converting something in some very boring mechanical manner like the typical of/to` pairs

Indeed. To be fair, if you convert something to a `task` (as in, the F# version of a hot task), it implies that you immediately start it. I don't think there is a way to convert it to a `task` that is not hot-started, and keep the same F#-task semantics. 

In fact, it was the whole point of `task {...}` to begin with: a hot-started computation expression for async stuff.

>  Mark this Resolved if you can live with the wording and/or can't thnk of a better one right now

👍 totally good! I like it.
@bartelink Looks good, I think your simplification of my original comments are an improvement. Besides, in general, I want the doc xml comments to be in the FSI files. All good!

>     /// Creates a Task<'U> that's completed successfully with the specified result.

As CompletedTask has the same wording as the FW docs, I followed suit and grabbed this from https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.threading.tasks.task.fromresult?view=net-8.0

Went with 

> Starts the `Async<'T>` computation, returning the associated `Task<'T>`

for both. I wanted a 'starts' or a 'running' or 'instance' in the wording to convey that in 90% of cases it kicks off work and is not just converting something in some very boring mechanical manner like the typical `of`/to` pairs

And I definitely agree that mentioning or lying about using StartImmediateAsTask is not going to help people regardless of whether they even know that that even is?

I note you have some `<returns>A <see cref="unit" /> <see cref="task" />.</returns>` usage in other places - I think it's clear and accurate there (even if I think I'd just say `Task<unit>` and be done with it had I not seen that way of writing it) e.g. https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Control.TaskSeq/blob/e76f9212934f8a5dd367e3fa42fb31ebb44b4bb6/src/FSharp.Control.TaskSeq/TaskSeq.fsi#L521

I'll let you Resolve this or pull rank

I think the non-generic task terminology edges it for me
- its explicit and unambiguous - saying what it does and when you need it
- does not lean on FP terms
- side effecting and/or the distinction between a `Task<unit>` or something with the same intent that happens to return `Task` for techical reasons are a not the point
- even if this was about a `Task<unit>` that had been upcasted to `Task` (or someone was ignoring the result intentionally by that mechanism), the original was a unit task
So I'll make it consistently 'non-generic' for now, but won't object/when if/when you flip all the cases to something else consistent

https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Control.TaskSeq/pull/249/commits/b88153a26cb81f4ba97cdeb659e1004b801dcaee

That's all fine when we are taking a running async.startchild and wanting to await it as a task. but the to/of for me is comfy and intuitive/expected with List vs Array. it gets more interesting with Array vs Seq where conversion is not even always possible. But starting a running task in the thread pool is just not the land of `of`/`to` IMO (whereas Async.ofTask and Task.toAsync is not as problematic as it's always about something that's already hot.
IIRC this all started from me wanting to get rid of a distracting `async'`, so we're probably fine to just leave it.
Also the real answer here is of course #141 / #139, but I don't have the bandwidth to really awake that bear right now
Definitely not pushing for startimmediateastask - anything starting in the new world can stick to task expr stuff

(edit: I thought GH would place this underneath your comments. It didn't. Sorry for the confusion, but it applies to this https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Control.TaskSeq/pull/249#discussion_r1553941027)

Back to `StartImmediateAsTask`: I've done a deep dive into the difference of `task { return! myAsync }` and `Async.StartImmediateAsTask myAsync`, and these methods are quite different.

The second entirely uses FSharp.Core and a bunch of extra allocations, while the first is more efficient and calls into the .NET library of task functions almost immediately. On the surface it seems that `return!` is more efficient.

Which means that we (probably) shouldn't use `StartImmediateAsTask` in the description, as it's not (entirely) correct.

As to `runAsync` etc, the idea here is to have conversion functions to/from Async as people are accustomed to conversion between like types in F#. But I'll keep it in mind as they do convey the "action" better.

Your suggestions got me thinking. Perhaps we should say here:

> Create a completed task from a value

> that might have not have the hot vs cold thing sorted out

Yes, and I like that. I mean, I like it that you try to improve on that message here. 

``` 
/// Runs the `Async<'T>` computation via `StartImmediateAsTask`, returning the associated `Task<'T>`
```

seems fair.

> that this in the general case will start work

What I meant was "it starts a running car". It is a double entendre. You either "start a car (and make it run)" or "continue running (if it already ran, but didn't stop yet)".

Same with asyncs, right? The typical `Async<'T>` instance is not running, until you call `StartAsTask` or something.

Either, you make it run, or you start it. But "start a running async" (start a running instance of an async) seems a bit odd to me... That's all ;)
Your choice ;)

I forgot what lib it was, but I know I didn't invent the term. I did a quick search, but searching for `unit`  and ` task` in F# repos (and GH not allowing string search) is like a needle in a haystack.

I'm fine either way. Though there's something to say that both `Task`  and `Task<unit>` could be consisered unit-tasks (or side-effect tasks or whatevs).
No no, it must stay as an extension member on the type. Comments are fine, but leave it otherwise as-is.

>The Async does not have to be running

If you're referring to things like an Async wrapping a Task or a StartChild, I'm more thinking about the fact that this in the general case will start work

> <suggestion>

Probably. I think of Async as a Builder, and any of the StartX things as doing a Create (or a Task.Run).
I'm trying to convey unambiguously to people that might have not have the hot vs cold thing sorted out and/or are not paying attention that this kicks off a new thing, an that continuations will be in the threadpool
The fact that the input is an Async<'T> is immaterial

Trying to cover both angles, starting from:

> `/// Immediately starts an Async<'T> and returns it as a Task<'T>`

I would say (if wanted to convey that it kicks off new work, and that the new work is a Task

`/// Starts the computation associated with the `Async<'T>` inline, returning the associated `Task<'T>`

But I don't like that; Maybe:

`/// Runs the `Async<'T>` computation via `StartImmediateAsTask`, returning the associated `Task<'T>`

**The main thing I'm trying to convey to someone sprikling `Async.toTask` around is that in the general case new parallel work is kicking off in the threadpool when you do this, even if technically we could be mapping a wrapped handle from a StartChild or AwaitTask**

what are these libraries?
I personally intuitively got what it meant and liked it when I first saw it.
But happy to flip all the 'non-generic X` to `unit-X` in this PR; my main desire is consistency...

Not entirely true (I think), as the `async` does not have to be running already. It will start the instance of the async, but it does not (quite) start a _running_ instance (as then it would already be running, right?).

Maybe this?
```suggestion
    /// Starts an instance of an Async<'T>, represented as a Task<'T>
```
lol! Nice catch 👍
Another good catch!
Good catch!

Actually, this is incorrect. The `Async` does not have to be running. Tasks are hot-started, asyncs are not. It is more likely it is not running. Maybe this?
```suggestion
    /// Immediately starts an Async<'T> and returns it as a Task<'T>
```
Note that the term "unit-task" is used in some other libraries. I'm also not really a fan of specifying something by mentioning the absence of something (but I also don't have a good alternative).
Yeah, all these "generic vs non-generic" versions of things in .NET are hard to explain simply. I've had several version of this text in the past. Each improvement is welcome! 👍

Yes, it's deliberate. It shadows `CompletedTask`, that exists by default in later versions of .NET. This way, people that use the later version of .NET will get the build-in version, but users that want to use it and are bound to .NET Standard or other older versions, can use this.

Hence mq quest for a workd like 'renders' or 'yields' to convey that while we are combining things, there is not really anything 'new' except in the sense that any function that does not return its input results in a 'new' value.

(about putting the function type DU inside the body)
I understand the sentiment, but I prefer not having this business logic in the body as it incurs an extra penalty and allocation (remember that value types are boxed as params in an object in this case). 

So, in general, I try to keep the DUs that declare the function type as far as reasonable on the fringes of the body, and I have been thinking of getting rid of them completely, or at least have them outside of the body of the `taskSeq`.

But I'll double-check the "best practices" here. I think it's bad practice to offer synchronous disposal of async resources, but let's check.

This fix can lead to backward compat issues, so we need to take it out for sure and treat separately.

This is a bug in F# (i.e., not raising the warning), we should not fix the mistake in the library by implementing an extra interface, we should fix F# instead. Some very old versions of this code used to have it.

> "The first element where the predicate returns false is returned,"

I was going to argue with this but I see now that it is technically correct. The point is that there is nothing special about the first item though - it just skips while it returns true. And then it returns the rest. Which includes the first item of the rest. And the rest of the rest. Including the very last item. if there is one.

>t says "it skips 0 or more elements", it does not skip the first one that fails.

You're probably correct - tbh I lost the will to parse it; it just feels very long winded, and I dont see whom explaining it in that manner actually helps...

----

Bottom line is that this level of over explanation is only justified if you're trying to explain it in terms of something that does have those factors in play.

i.e skipWhileInclusive.

----

TL;DR SkipWhile is really easy to explain if we just take the wording from Seq.takeWhile (which is not saddled with a contrived sibling :P)

yeah its releae mode only
I ran into one in Equinox so have a thing I would like to simplify but dont know how 
https://github.com/jet/equinox/blob/master/src/Equinox/Stream.fs#L33

Next proposal:
>skipWhile skips while the condition holds, but stops before the item that fails

thats overcomplicating matters compared to just saying it skips while the condition holds [, then returns the rest]

The only reason to talk in that way is if you have some other thing to go along with it...

>takeWhile takes while the condition holds, but stops before the item that fails

not sure the ", but stops before the item that fails" adds anything
but, I can see the before/after symmetry
but it feels contrived....

>takeWhileInclusive takes while the condition holds, but stops after the item that fails

while the impl does these things and it is a good way of explaining to someone thinking of the mechanism, its not as good as "like takeWhile but also yields the final item (that failed)"

>skipWhileInclusive skips while the condition holds, but stops after the item that fails

again I cant picture what I'd use it for

So I'm saying I like the first set of explanations more than the last one

But still dont see the what I'd use it for, and there is a tax in having stuff to trawl through and/or rule out if you are never going to use it

----

I get the collect id vs map stuff

But big diff between takeWhileWhileInclusive is that you can't roll your own as a one liner whereas trimming off one more after a skipWhile is relatively trivial.

The ultimatle reason I'm being pedantic is
a) no prior art
b) cant imagine using it
c) it makes it harder to explain the simple normal one

(afk for a bit, sorry for not having time to write it shorter)

But

>skipWhileInclusive skips while the condition holds, but also skips the first one that fails

this seems a clear explanation, and the table looks logical

but I still don't know what I use it for

Also, we should then improve the `Seq.fsi` comments, as in, return the favor to the community and improve there as well, as that is where most of this comes from.

(something I've been wanting to do for a long while, but... time...)

It says at L854:

```
A function that evaluates to false when no more items should be returned.
```

about the predicate function for `takeWhile`. What is the confusing bit? But if we want to improve existing wording on existing xml doc, we should open a separate pull request instead.

I do get the completeness/having the duals/inverses argument 

But in this case there are many problems:
- Seq does not have it
- The name is wierd
- I honestly cant think of an example concrete usage
- Its hard to explain a perfectly sane SkipWhile as being an opposite of something you cant picture

So I'm still not convinced :(

> skipWhile should not/does not return the first one that fails?

It says "it skips 0 or more elements", it does not _skip_ the first one that fails. Exactly like `Seq.skipWhile` does.

>It is not that simple. tail would raise on empty. Though drop 1 would be safe, I guess. But it depends on how far the skipWhile has gotten. Also, the same can be said for takeWhileInclusive, which is just an extra TaskSeq.head.

No, the reason for the inclusive is that we want one into the dropped bit - head is no use for that as you'd need to count what you saw, re-iterate skipping that many items etc ?

PS, if you take this from you:
![image](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Control.TaskSeq/assets/16015770/dfbe0cc5-0dd5-4e3d-b9bb-012ebbcf4139)

And then I fix it like this:
* skipWhile skips while the condition holds
* takeWhile takes while the condition holds
* takeWhileInclusive takes while the condition holds, but also takes the first one that fails
* skipWhileInclusive skips while the condition holds, but also skips the first one that fails

you can clearly see how they pair together.

Btw, this may be better?

* skipWhile skips while the condition holds, but stops _before_ the item that fails
* takeWhile takes while the condition holds, but stops _before_ the item that fails
* takeWhileInclusive takes while the condition holds, but stops _after_ the item that fails
* skipWhileInclusive skips while the condition holds, but stops _after_ the item that fails

> Amirite that >> TaskSeq.tail / >> TaskSeq.drop 1 would achieve the same semantic?

It is not that simple. `tail` would raise on empty. Though `drop 1` would be safe, I guess. But it depends on how far the `skipWhile` has gotten. Also, the same can be said for `takeWhileInclusive`, which is just an extra `TaskSeq.head`.

I feel like if we support `takeWhileInclusive`, we should support `skipWhileInclusive` for parity. The same has been done in many functions in both `Seq` and `AsyncSeq` space.

I needed the same truth table for `takeWhileInclusive`, I just never committed it. While working with the functions once you know that they are stable, is much easier. A simple call in FSI shows you what it does.

It's how I tested the edge cases that I wanted to support, by checking `Seq.take/takeWhile/skip/skipWhile` etc.

I'm not dismissing your point, but I find parity and orthogonality very important. Btw, as a comparison, likewise `Seq.map2` is also just `Seq.zip >> Seq.map`, and `Seq.concat` is just `Seq.collect id`, yet they do all exist.

Note this is inspired/provoked by a combo of two things:

1. spans of ... `list ... List.map |> List.map |> List.toSeq` perpetually coming up in code reviews

2. In Equinox, there's a place where the downstream logic requires a materialized result, but early on, the primary need is to know wheter it is empty or not. Only after some further processing are the actual items required.

There's also an Async angle - the function could be AsyncSeq in nature in that the processing might need to do Async things. But ultimately we need to materialize all the results, so taking an AsyncSeq would not be useful.

Based on this high level requirement, I said "that's a job for a `list`, they have neat comprehensions, are idiomatic and immutable" and convey the correct semantic - we need the full thing, and we'll traverse it in stages.

But, there's also a secondary factor - C# only provides seq comprehensions, and forcing Async rather than Task is bad news.

----

The solution?

Well, the lowest common denominator is seq, that would work naturally for C#, and `list` will naturally upcast to that

But, the real need is a) convey we need all the items (they are not going to be pulled incrementally so no point in implementing it lazily) b) with a quick way to check if the result is empty

So, the `list` + `seq` pair of APIs got changed to a single one that takes an array on the basis that:
- it removes the F# vs C# "slighly different interfaces" confusion/pointless variance (though there still is an Async vs Task divide)
- in F#, an array comprehension vs a `seq {` tends to be less messy
- in C# I'm sure they'll have an Array comprehension eventually, and adding an extension method to to a `Enumerable.ToArray` on the Seq is easy to do
- yes, an array is mutable, but in practice we can tell people "well then don't do that" if they actually mutate items (and most people don't think of arrays as mutable in that way) and/or eventually use `iarray` if something like that becomes universal

----

So, I _think_ that having a similar "not perfect but on balance the best choice" approach should be considered. `Async.Parallel` is an example of this (it takes a `seq` but then calls `Seq.toArray` immediately internally). For me, this would resolve the List vs Array vs ResizeArray thing, as long as the the semantics are clear and/or intuitive (I am guessing what they are here) i.e.:
- for a seq, its lazy
- for all the others they are upcast to seq, so don't go modifying/appending to the resizearray while there's a live iterator on it)

I held off on mentioning this earlier but it comes up in another issue....

This was the original .NET 4.0 set of Thread Pool helpers. All usage of `Task.Factory` has been shifted to `Task` helpers. In this specific case, the canonical way to express this is as a `Task.Run`. The `Run` verb is associated with a) starting stuff b) in the thread pool

Seaching StartAsTask vs StarImmediateTask, this seems to be the only useful hit https://github.com/fsharp/fslang-suggestions/issues/521

StartAsTask does a QueueAsync like Async.Start and Async.StartChild:
https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/blob/main/src/FSharp.Core/async.fs#L1200
https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/blob/main/src/FSharp.Core/async.fs#L1171

The only reason you'd prefer to queue rather than do it inline it is if you have a better thing to do on this thread right after you offload and/or you can. achieve better fairness by routing each piece of work through the thread pool each time?

I would think that `Async.StartImmediateAsTask` would be most efficient unless there's an express reason why it would not achieve some specified desired semantic - you're already in a context where you're running as a `task` in a valid context so no need to queue to be re-dispatched from the threadpool, potentially losing one's timeslice? (IIRC the logic and wrapping is identical aside from the extra dispatch hop)
But hard to argue against doing the same as `task` does
Yep, it does the same: https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/blob/d91b6c5c97accf363d135d1f99816410da4ec341/src/FSharp.Core/tasks.fs#L462

Perhaps this is better expressed in the text above, i.e. saying something like "The most fundamental difference is that `taskSeq` is built from the ground up against `System.Collections.Generic.IAsyncEnumerable` and `ValueTask`; `AsyncSeq` provides adapter functions to/from it, but the internals were written and evolved long before `IAsyncEnumerable` was defined and hence some aspects differ to varying degrees.

```suggestion
The [`AsyncSeq`][11] and `TaskSeq` libraries both operate on asynchronous sequences, but there are a few fundamental differences. The most notable being that the former _does not_ implement `IAsyncEnumerable<'T>`, though it does have a type of that name with different semantics (not surprising; it predates the definition of the modern one). Another key difference is that `TaskSeq` uses `ValueTask`s for the asynchronous computations, whereas `AsyncSeq` uses F#'s `Async<'T>`.
```

```suggestion
The [`AsyncSeq`][11] and `TaskSeq` library both operate on asynchronous sequences, but there are a few fundamental differences, most notably that the former _does not_ implement `IAsyncEnumerable<'T>`, but has its own same-named, but differently behaving type. 

There are more differences:

|                        | `TaskSeq`                                                   | `AsyncSeq`                                                           |
|------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Frameworks             | .NET 5.0+, NetStandard 2.1                                  | .NET 5.0+, NetStandard 2.0 and 2.1, .NET Framework 4.6.1+            |
| Underlying type        | `System.Collections.Generic.IAsyncEnumerable<'T>            | Its own type, also called `IAsyncEnumerable<'T>`, but not compatible |
| Implementation         | State machine (statically compiled)                         | No state machine, continuation style                                 |
| Semantics              | `seq`-like: on-demand                                       | `seq`-like: on-demand                                                |
| Support `let!`         | All task-like (async, task, valuetask, Task, or GetAwaiter) | `async` only                                                         |
| Support `yield!`       | `IAsyncEnumerable<'T>`, `AsyncSeq`, any sequence            | `AsyncSeq`                                                           |
| Support `for`          | `IAsyncEnumerable<'T>`, `AsyncSeq`, any sequence            | `AsyncSeq` any sequence                                              |
| Conversion to other    | `TaskSeq.toAsyncSeq`                                        | `AsyncSeq.toAsyncEnum`                                               |
| Conversion from other  | Implicit (yield!) or `TaskSeq.ofAsyncSeq`                   | `AsyncSeq.ofAsyncEnum`                                               |
| Recursion in `yield!`  | No (requires F# support, upcoming)                          | Yes                                                                  |
| Based on F# concept of | `task`                                                      | `async`                                                              |
| MoveNext operation     | `ValueTask`                                                 | Cold-started `Task`                                                  |
| Cancellation           | Implicit token governing iteration                          | Implicit token passed to each subtask                                |
| Performance            | Very high, negligible allocations                           | Slower, more allocations, due to using `async`                       |
| Behavior with `Return` | No allocations, no `Task` created                           | Allocated, a `Task` will be created                                  |
| Parallelism            | Possible with ChildTask, support will follow                | Supported explicitly                                                 |
|                        |                                                             |                                                                      |
```

After extensive research, the basic rule is: there is no rule. Both F# and BCL libs are inconsistent, sometimes throwing exceptions, sometimes not. We may throw exceptions at some point, but right now I prefer to be consistent with the CE. That is, I want this:

```F#
> let s: seq<int> = seq { do () } ;;
val s: seq<int>

> s.GetEnumerator().Current;;
val it: int = 0
```

to behave equal to this:

```f#
> let s: seq<int> = Seq.empty;;
val s: seq<int>

> s.GetEnumerator().Current;;
System.InvalidOperationException:  // this is not consistent in Seq
```

In other words: we will *not* throw an exception here for `taskSeq`. I may change this resolution, but for now, I think consistency is better, even though I agree in principal that throwing an exception isn't bad per se.
Btw, just do be clear, the public function remains in `TaskSeq.fs`. That file should contain all surface area public functions. Only the implementation moves. Sorry for being vague earlier.

>  what's wrong on having a large file with implementations?

Nothing. It’s a personal preference. Ease of reading, clear separation of multi purpose implementation from single purpose wrappers.

I find (very) large implementation files hard to navigate. But there’s something to say for either approach. In this project I chose simplicity (smaller files with some logical, somewhat arbitrary separation) over one big implementation file (like in AsyncSeq). Both have pros and cons, but in the end, you choose a direction and stick to it, or it becomes messy.

Yes. It’s nothing more than organisational. Also, we group like functions. Say we add `singletonAsync`, we’d process both in a single function in `TaskSeqInternal`.

This division is slightly arbitrary, but I like to keep the larger details separate from the lean and mean implementation file. In the end, there’s no functional reason other than organisation and style.

>  within an invalid state is an undefined operation, therefore invalid operation.

Undefinec and invalid are not interchangeable. Undefined means “we don’t tell you what to do, it’s the implementer’s choice”. Invalid means: “this operation is illegal”.

If `seq { yield 10 }` throws instead of `defaultof` I’ll have to check. I’ll also compare with other BCL libs. Consistency is key. From a contract perspective, either is fine.

Sure I can, but I wonder what's wrong on having a large file with implementations?
